speaker: interviewer
text: So after 10 or 15 minutes we will [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number four with Helen Mark. Please go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I did get a visitor one day, a gentleman lieutenant who came from Regensburg. And they had a lot of papers on the big board and said, get ready. We're leaving for Regensburg.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, but I have to go first. How long will it take you to get ready? Three days. He said no, half an hour. I say, but I have to go to Prague and get my money and everything. He said, no, either now or-- I said OK, better go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he put me in a GI uniform in the back of a car with three-star general. And he was sitting in front next to the driver. And we got to the border and he showed them the papers. They just saluted. And we left and went into Germany, and they didn't even care. And then when we were out they said, I want you to know-- all those papers on the board. There was nothing. They just don't know it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I got to Regensburg, and I went to General Lentz, who was occupying a very beautiful hunting lodge of a former prince [INAUDIBLE]. And I stayed there for a couple of weeks. And then I-- I got permission to go into the city in the hotel that was taken over by Third Army special services.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was allowed to live there and wear a uniform with Czechoslovakia on my sleeve, and work for the army. And I got a PX card. And I stayed there till 1947, because that's how long it took for me to get all of my papers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I finally was called to the consul, the American consul in Munich and everything was finished, he said get up. Raise your right hand. And I stood there and he said, where's your birth certificate? And I said I don't have one. I'm in Auschwitz. And he said, well how do I know you are Jewish? And he starts speaking Yiddish to me. Now, I didn't understand it. I never did speak it and I couldn't answer. He said, listen-- anybody can say they're Jewish and want to go to America.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said first go back and get me your birth certificate. So I went back, desperate, to Regensburg. And the commanding officer was very nice and just said, I'll call Vienna. They went in Vienna to the Jewish community, got a duplicate of my birth certificate, and sent it immediately with their special mail. I went back to Munich. But in the meantime, the boat that I was supposed to go, the displaced person transport had left Bremerhaven, so I had to wait a few weeks. But they-- I got my visa. And my uncle in New York had sent me an affidavit, and that's how I got my quota number. So I went on that displaced person transport. But when I came into the barracks where we had to assemble, they told me to go in-- it used to be a German army barracks. And they said, go to barracks so-and-so, and room number so-and-so. I went. And there were again two-tier bunks. And in the middle was a table and two benches with the magazines lying there, but nobody was in the room. But every bunk except one had little luggage on it. So I went to the empty one and put my little case on it and sat down at the table.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the door opens a little bit later and a woman comes in-- riding breeches, very tough-looking-- and says-- boots, high boots-- which bunk is open? I said, they're all taken as far as I can see. And she stood with her legs apart like this. And I looked at her and I realized she was one of my former SS guards.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she made the big mistake to put her-- take my bag off, put it on the table, and put her bag on my bunk. I didn't say a word. I walked out. I went down to the barracks where the CIC officer was and told him what happened. He said, out of the question. They are so-- you know, for hours interviewed everybody. I said look, she knows as much about camp as I do. She was in there with me. She can tell you all the stories, but I tell you she is an SS woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: So finally, I convinced him and he picked her up. And they interviewed her and grilled her for hours. And they finally found out that she is the SS woman I said she was. Her husband was already in America. They were going to get him back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what they did was say that they're from the German Polish corridor where you couldn't get any papers, because everything was bombed out. And as far as interviewing about camp, they both knew exactly the same as I did. So I really wanted to call that consul in Munich and say you were right. I apologized in my mind to him, because I was so mad that he didn't believe me, I'm Jewish. But so finally I went and I came to--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of this SS woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I have no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it feel like for you to be able to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Furious. To tell you the truth, I was furious, because they can't-- you know they can't change. They stand a certain way and talk a certain way. And they are arrogant and they are these-- feet like this apart, and I'm, you know, here. But I was so glad that I was going and that the whole thing was over that you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I only know they picked her up and put her in prison. And what happened later, I don't know. And he promised me, he said, for sure they're picking up her husband in New York and bring him back. So I don't know. I went happily to New York. My uncle picked me up in New York. And I saw-- you know, I had some family and old friends. And in New York is a pastry coffee shop, which belongs till recently to a Czech gentleman who was married to a Viennese. And whoever came from Europe and wanted to find, like we did, find friends from Vienna or Prague, everybody said all you have to do is go to the Eclair. That's where you'll find them. And that's what I did. Found old school friends, found friends from ice skating, found you know. Which helped tremendously, because when you come and you find friends who are established and who have lived there for a few years and have a home, it makes things much easier. And there I met an old school friend of mine who worked in a place where my future husband worked. And so one day she came and said, you know there's such a nice Viennese guy. I'm going to bring him along. He doesn't know anybody from our old Viennese group. And she did, and I think four months later I got married, five months-- June, five months later. And well, I worked for-- when I came and needed a job, there was a dentist who was my family dentist in Prague before the war, and he had a very good practice in New York. And a lot of his patients were former patients from Prague and also a lot of Viennese people came there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he needed somebody who spoke Czech, and German, and English. And that's how I got my first job. And I hate the dentist and dental offices. And said, well, I go there for a few weeks till I find something better, but I need to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I stayed 24 years till I moved to California, because my husband had been working for advertising agency in New York and came out here on business and loved it. And he was a big tennis player. He loved tennis. And when he saw you play tennis all-year round here, that sold him-- Los Angeles, already. And the company that he came to talk to here wanted him to come out and take over the media department. So we both had no family in New York and nobody. His mother had passed away, his father had passed. So we decided that New York was already very hard to live. It was dangerous, it was dirty, it was really no longer the city that we used to love. And when we decided we should try, I said yes. So we moved out here January 1, 1970. And he worked for this company for a while. And then everybody said, you know what you do for others you can do really for yourself. And he wasn't very happy there anyway. And so he opened his own agency. And I worked first for a hotel, restaurant, and bartenders union here, and then I-- for about five years. And then I stopped and joined my husband and learned the business and ran the company with him, and loved every minute of it. And-- until 1987, and then John passed away of cancer, and I sold the company and retired.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that is, more or less, the end of my story. Did you ever have any children, Helen?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I had-- no. See, in Terezin, when I was married, I got pregnant. And everybody who got pregnant had to have an abortion. They either didn't let you have babies-- and at that time the commandant, the SS leader of the camp changed. And I asked for permission to have the child, and he granted the permission. No, the old one. I'm sorry. The old one granted the permission. And then it changed and another SS leader came and he revoked all permissions.

speaker: interviewee
text: So by the time it was ripe, I had to have a forced labor, you know, made-- and taken the child. And I never had-- I never dared to try again, because I didn't know-- no doctor would guarantee me that it would be OK after that. So I had no children.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had adopted a little girl in Terezin, but she was sent to Switzerland. When I left, you know, I left her in the-- little girl. She was from Berlin and I thought I'd pick her up afterwards. But then when I came back, I went to Terezin, and they had sent the children to Switzerland with the Red Cross, about three, four months before the end of the war. And nobody knew where they were, couldn't find them. I went to the Red Cross and-- Swiss, Swede, everywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, but at least she was in Switzerland and she was safe, and grew up there, I guess. So I didn't-- From your experiences in concentration camps during the war, is there anything that you learned that you would like to pass on to future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I tell you one thing, don't give up hope ever. And you know I have seen it over and over again, you have to-- you have to hope. You have to feel that you can do it. Don't think, I can't take that. Don't ever do that, because then you're lost. And if you think, you know, nothing, it's never going to end, you're-- you can't make it. And I've seen young, strong young men, and I've seen friends of mine, women who sort of gave up, and none of those came back. Because you have to-- you have to have the hope, and the willpower, and the-- maybe not everybody can do it. Maybe I was very lucky that I said no, I'm going to do it. They're not going to kill me. And each time I was lucky. Once I picked up a stick, once they closed the gas chamber ahead of me. So somewhere, I think, you have to-- you have to have the will to live, and you have to have hope, and you have to not give up. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and be kind to each other, because it's [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a tough time. But I'll tell you one thing, I hope that we'll never be put to the test again how much we can take, because you have no idea how much a human being can take. But I don't want to ever find out again. It's very, very heartbreaking. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And then, I'm here. You know-- I can't complain. I'm here. I'm grateful I was with my mother as long as I was. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm grateful I had the chance of a new life. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's all I can say. Thank you very much, Helen.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. This is my mother, Ani Bellack. And the picture was taken in Prague in our home in 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father, Robert Bellack. And the picture was taken sometime in 1936 or 1937.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Vienna, Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was killed in a camp in Salerno, Italy in 1940. This is a picture of me, Helen Mark, and it was taken in Prague in 1940. This is a picture of my husband, John Mark, and it was taken in Los Angeles in 1975. This is a picture of me taken in Los Angeles, 1975. This is a picture made in Terezin by an inmate. And it is a takeoff on the opera and has to do with ghetto life, and making fun of the ghetto police. And just a charming little painting.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is another picture out of that series. And the words from the arias of the opera are used for the text of them. And it is, why shouldn't we all be happy? What it is, is made for a conductor who was in Terezin.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the pictures were made by a friend of his, a painter, and given to him as a souvenir of the performance of

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a picture that belongs in the same series. And its two lovers who meet in a place where they're not supposed to be, but the ghetto police is helping them and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] making it easier for the lovers to meet. And that's what it says. Why shouldn't we be happy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, here is already the end. We're going home. At least that's what they hope for and say that good things have happened, succeeded. So they are going home on the train. Well, this is the cover of that map which should contain the paintings, and is showing who it is made for. And the way I got them was in New York. A gentleman came who brought them from Prague and was a patient in our office. And he gave them to my boss, who was from Prague. And my boss gave them to me because he thought they mean much more to me than they could ever mean to him. And the way they were saved was that the lot of paintings that were made by very, very good artists in Terezin, were hidden in the walls of the army barracks. And after the war, some people who survived or who knew about it went and got those things out of the walls and brought them back. And so was also this series saved in the walls of one of the army barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Terezin?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Terezin, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Helen.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.
